comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the visualization of land value in cities, with commenters debating the accuracy, readability, and implications of the maps shown. Several highlight the economic principles behind land value gradients and the fiscal impact of urban versus suburban areas, while others critique the visualization style and data sources.

comments.author: svcphr
comments.text:
> "1. People have wildly incorrect intuitions about where land value is concentrated"

Fwiw this sort of land value gradient has been studied in economics for ages. See papers on monocentric city model, going back to Alonso (1964), Muth (1969), and Mills (1967). Or even further back, von Thünen was talking back in 1826 about how land values spike as you get closer to the marketplace.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a well-informed historical and theoretical context for the land value gradient, grounding the discussion in established economic literature.

comments.author: Night_Thastus
comments.text:
The other thing people often forget is that the 'land value' is also a measure of the city's well-being.

Those big spikes you see in the center? They cost very little for the city to maintain, and generate oodles of tax money.

Those big, wide areas out towards the fringes? They generate next to no tax income and cost a lot to maintain.

The urban subsidizes the sub-urban. The sub-urban lifestyle would be completely impossible without the ultra-dense urban centers. If planners and citizens don't keep that in mind, you can easily end up with an insolvent city budget that is bleeding from maintaining all the utilities and roads stretching out to the exterior.
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully explains the fiscal dynamics between urban and suburban areas, emphasizing the financial strain of maintaining sprawling infrastructure.

comments.author: AnthonyMouse
comments.text:
This is mostly a result of zoning, isn't it? The high land value areas are the ones where you're less prohibited from building taller buildings. If the thing people actually want is indoor space then the piece of land where you can build a skyscraper is worth a lot more than the one which is limited to a single family home.

Someone should probably tell the homeowners with a high ratio of land to house who like to see their property values increase.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately connects zoning regulations to land value, highlighting how building height restrictions impact property worth.

comments.author: cheriot
comments.text:
Now map property taxes per acre and tell me who's subsidizing who in this country. Urban3 has been doing the work: https://codesigncollaborative.org/urban-revolution-through-d...

Municipal costs per resident are effectively the inverse of these maps because the more spread out people are the more roads, pipes, etc are required to reach them.
comments.analysis: This adds valuable nuance by focusing on tax revenue and municipal costs, supporting the fiscal argument about urban-suburban subsidies with external data references.

comments.author: korkoros
comments.text:
The overall claim is true - yes put it on a map.

But I'm not a fan of these particular maps because the use of 3d makes them harder to read. The isometric view and rotation away from north at the top break conventions that people use to orient themselves in the map and connect it to their lived experiences on the ground. I'm reasonably familiar with NYC geography, and I could not immediately recognize the landscape I was looking at in these maps. Ironically, it was only because I already knew the answer to the question that I could do so: "oh that huge green spike must be Manhattan".

I think a 2d choropleth map with a diverging color scale centered on the mean value would work better.
comments.analysis: The critique of visualization techniques is valid, emphasizing usability and familiarity in map design to improve comprehension.

comments.author: ancillary
comments.text: Is "land value" the right term here? The NYC example uses assessed property value, which I think is a function of both the land under a property and the building itself. In that case, these "taller means more valuable" graphics are at least partially reflecting the fact that a tall building is probably more valuable than the short one next to it?
comments.analysis: This comment raises an important point about data interpretation, cautioning against conflating land value with total property value.

comments.author: chaos_emergent
comments.text: The writer mentioned that people's intuitions about the distribution of land and where it's most valuable are wildly off, what exactly are people's intuitions that run counter to the data presented? It seems fairly intuitive to me that property values, as you get closer to an urban center
comments.analysis: While questioning the premise is reasonable, the comment lacks detail or evidence about the supposed incorrect intuitions.

comments.author: paulluuk
comments.text:
This is great, and it also feels like a great way to answer the question "Where should I buy a house if I want to be close to the center but not in the expensive area?".

> Let’s play a guessing game. How much more valuable is land in Manhattan than in the Bronx? Take a guess, then scroll down for the answer.

As someone who has never been in New York and doesn't live in the US, I knew beforehand that I would fail this test very hard, haha.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a user perspective on the utility of the visualization but does not add technical or factual critique.

comments.author: etiennebausson
comments.text:
How much the land is worth is only one of the parameters.

Notoriously, the maintenance cost for suburbs and their infrastructure is significantly lower than the tax they bring. Shouldn't that be a major point un tax decisions?
comments.analysis: The claim that suburban maintenance costs are lower than taxes collected contradicts common urban planning findings and lacks supporting evidence here.

comments.author: xnx
comments.text: Probably fun to make but harder to read compared to a bar chart.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a superficial critique without elaboration or constructive suggestions.

comments.author: Drunk_Engineer
comments.text: Nice idea, except the actual mapper site requires a google login to view.
comments.analysis: This practical observation highlights a barrier to access but does not engage with the content or analysis of the visualization.

comments.author: jonathanberger
comments.text: Note that the site that generated these does not support any San Francisco Bay Area cities. I learned this only after being forced to "Sign in with Google".
comments.analysis: This comment points out geographic and access limitations of the tool, useful for potential users but not analytical about the data itself.

comments.author: the_sleaze_
comments.text:
> Show an elected official

What is the problem this visualization seeks to make obvious? Is it just neat to think about and make?
comments.analysis: This comment is dismissive without engaging meaningfully with the visualization's purpose or content.
